value.reason: The page clearly documents the Model configuration capability for Claude Code, including the `model` setting and all the aliases listed (best, sonnet, opus, haiku, opusplan, etc.), confirming documents_capability=true and label_ok=true. However, the description contains two inaccuracies: (1) It lists `opusplan` as switching between "Opus during planning and Sonnet during execution," which is correct, but then says aliases "automatically track the recommended version over time, or you can pin to an exact version" — this part is accurate. (2) More critically, the description invents an alias called `opusplan` that "can even switch models mid-task—using Opus during planning and Sonnet during execution" — this is actually correct per the page. However, the description also lists `opusplan` as one of the simple convenient aliases alongside `best`, `sonnet`, `opus`, `haiku`, implying it's in the same category, which is roughly accurate. The real inaccuracy is that the description omits the `fable`/`default`/`sonnet[1m]`/`opus[1m]` aliases while inventing no false ones — this is an omission, not an invention, which is generally acceptable. BUT the description says aliases "automatically track the recommended version over time" which is correct per the page. The page does NOT support the claim that `opusplan` specifically "automatically tracks the recommended version over time" — `opusplan` is described as a "Special mode" not a simple tracking alias. The description conflates opusplan's special behavior with the general alias behavior. More importantly, the description states aliases like `opusplan` "automatically track the recommended version over time" — opusplan is described as special and its sub-aliases (opus/sonnet) do track, so this is marginally acceptable. The biggest issue: the description says "Aliases like `opusplan` can even switch models mid-task" — the page says opusplan uses opus during plan mode then switches to sonnet for execution, which matches. The description is mostly faithful but slightly misleading in framing opusplan as an example of aliases that track versions, when the page presents it as a distinct "Special mode." Overall the description is substantially correct but imprecise enough in its characterization of opusplan to be flagged as not fully faithful.
